# My approach (generated by Me)

Since this is time boxed to 90 minutes, I wanted to get straight to the point and trim as much fat from an LLM back-and-forth: I read the brief a few times over and distilled it as thoroughly as possible, in terms of how I understood it (making sure to paste in direct requirements so those weren't lost). I'm working with Opus 4.8 to work out the best path forward in phases, with the early phases being dedicated to the "impact" rubric itself and the later ones to the dashboard application. 

Overall, the approach that I had in mind was some sort of multi-faceted weighted average, with certain criteria weighing more than others. I wanted to be careful not to push the conversation one way or another, so I kept things fairly open-ended. One thing I already had in mind before starting was ensuring code deletions are counted heavily, since engineers coming into codebases and removing things (i.e. making them more efficient, less bloated, etc.) are often more impactful than those simply pushing +10,000 line commits with 0 deletions (new repos, documentation, etc.). Opus actually anticipated this and delivered it immediately as part of the rubric, as well as a tight, 5-pillar weighted grading system as part of a detailed plan – I'm satisfied with this approach for now, and would rather get straight into the code and tweak the scoring later.

In terms of the app itself, my priority is accessibility and themeability, so I didn't want to be too opinionated on architecture. Opus suggested Vite/React/Tailwind/Vercel – good enough for me; I don't personally use Vercel much for deployments so Claude Code correctly identified I don't have the CLI installed, and instead opted for Cloudflare via Wrangler (which I do use).

After I've got my Opus plan, I take it to Claude Code and run it, giving me an opportunity to verify the validity as this new instance of Claude goes through it. In a real-world scenario, I'd probably try to break this down into smaller tasks that I can hand off to Sonnet, potentially in a Ralph loop – but given the short time window I'm willing to burn some extra tokens just to get through stuff more quickly. One thing I want to note that I didn't preempt: posthog's repo is enormous, so it would've been beneficial to think through the approach on how to slice it up for more efficient digestion; no matter now, thankfully Opus in Claude Code flagged this as a potential issue and suggested using graphQL vs. simple REST queries.
